title: Factory Astro integration for knowledge artifact collections
narrative: Factory integrates with Astro static site generator to publish knowledge artifacts as content collections. The system defines five collection types using Zod schemas for runtime validation: specs (work definitions), patterns (reusable solutions), lessons (learned insights), reject narratives (failure documentation), and anti-patterns (problematic approaches). Each collection has tailored schemas - specs include workflow controls like effort and gate_profile with sensible defaults; lessons track confidence scores and source attribution; reject narratives categorize failures by severity; anti-patterns require evidence counts to establish pattern validity. The validateForAstro function provides a unified validation interface that routes to the appropriate schema, returning structured success/failure results. The generateAstroConfig function dynamically produces Astro configuration code defining all five collections with their schemas, enabling Factory to maintain a single source of truth for schema definitions used both at runtime and by Astro's build system. This architecture allows Factory to both validate artifacts during pipeline execution and publish them as searchable, browsable documentation.
facts: ["Five Zod schemas define Astro content collections: spec, pattern, lesson, reject-narrative, and anti-pattern","Spec schema defaults: effort=standard, gate_profile=standard, reject_policy=iterate, nl_interface=true, lessons=true","Lesson collection tracks confidence (0-1 range), source_run ID, source_gate, and created_at timestamp","Reject narrative collection uses severity enum: low, medium, high, critical for categorizing failures","Anti-pattern collection requires evidence_count (minimum 1 integer) and source_runs array for traceability","Pattern collection requires name, tags, domain, and description as mandatory fields","validateForAstro function routes frontmatter validation to appropriate schema based on collection name","generateAstroConfig produces Astro content collection configuration code as a string"]

title: Updated implementation plan with Grant's branch analysis and specific mod_security approach
narrative: The primary session updated the implementation plan with detailed specifications based on examining Grant's feature/ip-throttling branch. The plan now provides concrete technical details for Agent 4's work: branch name (feature/modsecurity-session-ratelimit), package installation method (apt-get install libapache2-mod-security2), configuration file location (conf.d/security.conf), and test file pattern to follow. This refinement transforms the plan from conceptual to executable with specific file names, package names, and technical justifications grounded in the actual codebase analysis.

title: Story 1.4 Specification: Init Workflow API Key Validation and Shell Completions
narrative: Story 1.4 specification loaded defining API key validation and shell completion generation for the init workflow. The story implements dual-stage ANTHROPIC_API_KEY validation: fast format checking (sk-ant- prefix validation) catches typos instantly, followed by lightweight API connectivity test to verify the key works. Validation results and timestamp are cached in ~/.cache/_factory-core/api-key-validation.json to avoid repeated network calls. Critical security requirement NFR-17 enforced: the API key must never appear in any output, log, error message, or telemetry - masking/redaction applied everywhere. Missing or invalid keys block init with clear error messages including setup instructions and Anthropic console link. Shell completion generation supports fish, bash, and zsh formats using the operator's chosen binary name from config.yaml, including all registered commands and subcommands. Completions are offered during init but optional. Implementation includes 4 tasks: API key validation with caching and masking, shell completion generation, integration into init flow, and comprehensive tests. Direct dependencies on Stories 1.1 (error types), 1.2 (XDG cache paths), and 1.3 (init orchestration).

title: Factory Development Infrastructure Architecture Finalized
narrative: Factory's development infrastructure architecture has been finalized after evaluating Steve's beads versus beads_rust. The decision favors beads_rust as a temporary bridge solution because Steve's beads is undergoing active Dolt server mode migration with port 3306 conflicts, has 276k lines of Go code, and is removing features like bd export. In contrast, beads_rust uses SQLite, maintains non-invasive git integration, and provides JSON robot mode for agent integration. Brian's requirement for a stable interface drove this choice, as Claude Code TaskList proved too volatile and YAML files get forgotten, while the beads data model has proven reliable in practice. The infrastructure will use Claude Code's worktree isolation mode for per-agent tasks, with a merge protocol where the lead context validates builds, tests, and lints before merging branches directly to main, creating git audit trails instead of PRs. A code-review agent validates diffs before merge as a review gate. The dispatch system relies on beads_rust's ready-work detection to identify unblocked stories and spawn agents for available work. Story 1.1 must land first as foundational scaffolding for all subsequent branches. Additionally, extensive research findings from Jeffrey Emanuel's work have been selectively adopted including Gate 0 injection defense with ACIP trust preambles, UBS scanner for Gate 4, memory maturity enums from CASS, progressive disclosure patterns from claude-mem, DuckDB cost attribution from Vibe Cockpit, delta magnitude metrics from APR, and enhanced pattern library typing. The plan explicitly rejects SLB two-person rules, Meta Skill dependencies, and MCP Agent Mail for MVP scope control. Factory will build its own Dolt-backed dependency system at Story 1.11 as the permanent solution.

title: ZeroClaw Library API Surface and Module Visibility
narrative: Examined zeroclaw's lib.rs revealing the library's public API surface and encapsulation strategy. The library enforces the same strict code quality standards as the binary with #![forbid(unsafe_code)] and clippy::pedantic lints ensuring all library code maintains zero unsafe blocks. Module visibility is carefully structured with 13 public modules (agent, channels, config, coordination, gateway, goals, hooks, memory, observability, peripherals, providers, rag, runtime, tools) available to external consumers who want to embed zeroclaw as a library, while 19 implementation modules remain internal via pub(crate) visibility (approval, auth, cost, cron, daemon, doctor, hardware, health, heartbeat, identity, integrations, migration, multimodal, onboard, security, service, skills, tunnel, util). This separation allows zeroclaw to function both as a standalone CLI application and as a library for building custom AI agent applications with programmatic access to core primitives. Command enums like ServiceCommands (daemon lifecycle: Install/Start/Stop/Restart/Status/Uninstall) and ChannelCommands are centrally defined in lib.rs and re-exported to main.rs, maintaining single source of truth for CLI interface definitions while supporting serialization (Serialize, Deserialize) for potential RPC or configuration use cases. The public API surface exposes high-level primitives for agent execution, channel communication, provider abstraction, tool execution, memory management, and observability, while hiding internal implementation details like authentication flows, cost tracking, cron internals, daemon management, security policies, and service orchestration.

title: Fixed Linq v3 webhook payload parser to prevent silent message drops
narrative: PR #1852 addresses a critical bug where Linq v3 webhook messages were silently dropped despite the gateway returning 200 OK responses. Linq's webhook version 2026-02-03 introduced a breaking change, restructuring the payload from flat fields to nested objects across multiple critical fields: sender phone, bot message indicators, chat identifiers, and message parts. The existing parser only understood the legacy flat structure, so while webhooks were successfully received, no data could be extracted from the new v3 format, causing complete message loss without any error indication. The fix updates `parse_webhook_payload()` to attempt v3 nested paths first before falling back to legacy flat paths, ensuring both new and old webhook versions work correctly. Debug logging was added to surface missing sender or parts data for future troubleshooting. The implementation includes comprehensive test coverage with 6 new v3-specific tests while maintaining all existing legacy tests, totaling 29 passing tests. Validation included real webhook captures and end-to-end testing through the complete pipeline: live Linq webhook → cloudflared tunnel → ZeroClaw gateway → v3 parser → message extraction.
facts: ["PR #1852 created to fix silent message drops when using Linq webhook version 2026-02-03 (v3)","Linq v3 restructured payload from flat to nested: `data.from` → `data.sender_handle.handle`, `data.is_from_me` → `data.sender_handle.is_me`, `data.chat_id` → `data.chat.id`, `data.message.parts` → `data.parts`","Gateway accepted webhook POSTs with 200 OK but parser extracted nothing from v3 structure, silently dropping all messages","`parse_webhook_payload()` updated to try v3 nested paths first, fall back to legacy flat paths for backward compatibility","Added debug logging when sender or message parts are missing to aid future debugging","6 new tests covering v3 format scenarios: text message, outbound skip, outbound-only skip, is_me skip, unauthorized sender, multiple parts","All 29 tests pass including unchanged legacy-format tests, verified against real Linq v3 webhook capture from issue #1845","PR #1852 replaces PR #1846 after clean rebase onto dev branch, fixes issue #1845"]

title: Story 1.7 specification created for CLI-to-daemon HTTP client over Unix socket
narrative: Created implementation specification for Story 1.7 establishing the CLI-to-daemon communication layer over Unix sockets. The client in src/cli/client.ts implements JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol for request/response exchanges, using Bun's fetch API with the unix parameter to connect to ~/.cache/_factory-core/factory.sock without exposing network ports. JSON-RPC request format wraps API calls in a standard envelope with jsonrpc version 2.0, method field identifying the endpoint (e.g., "runs.submit"), params object containing request data, and unique id for correlation. Responses follow the JSON-RPC 2.0 specification with either a result object containing data and metadata or an error object with numeric code, human-readable message, and optional details. SSE (Server-Sent Events) streaming support enables real-time event delivery for features like log tailing, with a custom parser extracting event type, data payload, and id from SSE frame format and exposing an async iterator interface for non-blocking consumption. Daemon availability detection implements two-stage verification: checking socket file existence at the expected path and attempting connection to distinguish between daemon-not-running and crashed-daemon-with-stale-socket scenarios, providing actionable error messages like "Factory daemon is not running. Start with `<bin> daemon start`" or "Factory daemon socket exists but is not responding. The daemon may have crashed. Try `<bin> daemon start`". Unix socket communication provides implicit filesystem-permission-based authentication per NFR-21, restricting access to users with appropriate file permissions without requiring separate authentication mechanisms or exposing TCP ports. The DaemonClient class exports a clean interface for command handlers, integrating with the formatter system from Story 1.5 to transform JSON-RPC responses according to the --format flag before display. Anti-pattern warnings emphasize keeping the JSON-RPC implementation minimal without external libraries since it's a simple JSON envelope, avoiding retry logic for daemon-not-running since it requires user action, maintaining local-only access without TCP binding for MVP scope, using async iteration for SSE streams to keep the CLI responsive to Ctrl+C interrupts, and maintaining complete decoupling from daemon-side code to prevent accidental dependencies.

title: Documented beads CLI machine-readable output with universal --json flag and bd ready agent workflow
narrative: Analyzed beads CLI reference documentation revealing comprehensive --json support across all commands with explicit recommendation for programmatic agent use. The bd ready command provides core agent workflow capability by returning unblocked, unassigned issues suitable for claiming. Import functionality supports JSONL with sophisticated orphan handling (allow/resurrect/skip/strict modes) and dry-run preview. Critical finding: --robot flag documented in beads_rust does NOT exist in beads CLI reference, representing a differentiation point between implementations. Similarly, no --format flag exists. Another key architectural difference: beads syncs via Dolt remotes rather than explicit export commands, contrasting with beads_rust's explicit sync --flush-only and --import-only operations. title: Swap Space Utilization Critical at 91% with 9.3GB of 10GB Used
narrative: Swap space analysis reveals critical memory pressure with 91% of allocated 10GB swap actively used. The system is maintaining 9.3GB of swapped-out memory on disk, representing data that couldn't fit in the 18GB physical RAM. With only 920MB swap space remaining, the system approaches its maximum virtual memory capacity. Encrypted swap (standard on macOS) adds CPU overhead for every swap operation, compounding the performance impact of the 29+ million cumulative swap I/O operations observed. The high swap utilization directly correlates with the 9.7GB wired memory keeping over half of physical RAM locked and unavailable for active applications. When combined with the data volume at 91% disk capacity (41GB free), the system has limited room to expand swap files if needed. This creates a critical bottleneck: physical RAM is 94% utilized with 9.7GB locked as wired, swap is 91% full, and disk space constrains further swap growth. Applications requiring additional memory must wait for pages to be compressed, purged, or swapped, causing the performance degradation observed in high load averages and system responsiveness.

narrative: Investigation reverse-engineered the CsrSessionData cookie encoding format to determine if cookie values could be decoded to reveal authenticated CSR identity. title: Pi Subagent Extension: Reviewer Agent with Defense-in-Depth Security Model
narrative: The reviewer agent specification demonstrates pi's layered security approach for sub-agents with elevated tool access. Unlike the planner agent which only has read tools (read, grep, find, ls), the reviewer includes bash access needed for git operations during code review workflows. However, the agent instructions implement policy-based constraints explicitly limiting bash usage to read-only git commands (git diff, git log, git show), with a critical security note acknowledging that tool permissions may not be perfectly enforceable at the technical level. This defense-in-depth model assumes that technical restrictions alone cannot guarantee safety, so agent instructions provide an additional layer of constraint. The review workflow mirrors human code review practices: first examine what changed via git diff, then read the affected files, finally analyze for issues. The structured output format enables downstream consumers (human reviewers or other agents) to quickly triage findings by severity: Critical issues block merging, Warnings should be addressed before merge, Suggestions can be deferred. The requirement for specific file paths and line numbers makes findings actionable rather than abstract. This pattern of combining tool access with instruction-based constraints allows flexibility (reviewer can use git commands) while maintaining safety boundaries (no builds, no modifications), acknowledging that LLMs may not always follow instructions perfectly but defense-in-depth reduces risk.

title: Session Identifier Separation Confirmed - visitor_id Distinct from JSESSIONID
narrative: Investigation confirmed that visitor_id and JSESSIONID are completely separate session tracking mechanisms with no direct cross-reference in the database. The smoking gun JSESSIONID (node05p3qwzlq19k1o4w8gq1vln8q100714.node0) does not appear anywhere in the user_activities table's visitor_id column, and the format differences make clear these serve different purposes. Visitor_id likely originates from analytics or page visit tracking (page_visit_sessions table), while JSESSIONID handles Jetty application session management. This creates an attribution challenge: while ROPERKI1 emerges as prime suspect based on temporal correlation and persistent session activity from the WSFG NAT IP, there's no database field directly linking ROPERKI1's visitor_id to the specific JSESSIONID seen in Apache logs submitting fraudulent applications. The investigation must now explore JettySessionIds or JettySessions tables (not yet queried) which should contain JSESSIONID-to-user mappings based on Jetty's JDBC session configuration, or examine application/container logs that might log both identifiers together.

title: Preset resolution translates symbolic presets to concrete runtime configurations
narrative: Factory's preset system translates symbolic configuration names into concrete runtime parameters across three dimensions. The effort preset controls computational resources: quick mode provides minimal resources (1 iteration, 50k tokens/agent) for fast feedback, standard mode balances speed and thoroughness (3 iterations, 200k tokens/agent), and tenacious mode maximizes quality (5 iterations, 500k tokens/agent) with correspondingly large total run budgets (200k, 1M, 3M respectively). The gate profile preset configures quality gates with escalating strictness: quick disables coverage requirements and treats lints as warnings while catching only critical security issues; standard enforces 80% coverage, lint errors, and high-severity security checks; strict demands 95% coverage and flags medium-severity security issues. The reject policy preset governs failure handling: fail_fast stops immediately on any gate failure without retries, iterate attempts up to 3 retries to fix issues autonomously, and escalate adds operator notification after exhausting retries. The resolveSpecPresets function provides one-stop resolution of all three preset types with schema-aligned defaults (standard/standard/iterate), ensuring specs always have complete configuration even when fields are omitted. This architecture allows spec authors to use human-friendly preset names while the runtime operates on precise numerical budgets and boolean flags.
facts: ["EffortBudget defines maxIterations, tokenBudgetPerAgent, and totalRunBudget for three effort levels","Quick effort: 1 iteration, 50k tokens per agent, 200k total run budget","Standard effort: 3 iterations, 200k tokens per agent, 1M total run budget","Tenacious effort: 5 iterations, 500k tokens per agent, 3M total run budget","GateConfig specifies coverageThreshold percentage, lintStrictness (warn/error), and minimum securitySeverity","Gate profiles scale from quick (0% coverage, warnings, critical-only) to strict (95% coverage, errors, medium+)","RejectConfig controls retryOnFailure boolean, maxRetries count, and escalateToOperator flag","Reject policies range from fail_fast (no retries) through iterate (3 retries) to escalate (3 retries + operator escalation)","resolveSpecPresets combines all three preset resolutions with defaults: standard effort, standard gate, iterate reject"]

title: IAM Policy Confirms Public Read Access via allUsers Binding
narrative: Retrieved IAM policy from gs://crowdstrike/ bucket confirming the root cause of the public exposure. The policy contains a binding that grants roles/storage.objectViewer to the special identifier "allUsers", which in GCP IAM means any user on the internet, authenticated or not. This permission allows anyone who knows the bucket URL to list and download all objects, including the sensitive Keycloak keystore and Jenkins workspace data. The bucket resides in the provision-general-operations project and uses legacy IAM roles (legacyBucketOwner, legacyBucketReader, legacyObjectOwner, legacyObjectReader) indicating the permissions were configured before the bucket was migrated to uniform bucket-level access. The Storage Transfer Service account has both objectAdmin (full object control) and legacyBucketReader (bucket metadata access) permissions, which were necessary for the July 12, 2024 transfer job that copied Jenkins workspace data. The public binding was likely added intentionally to make Crowdstrike installers easily accessible without authentication, but the administrator failed to restrict this access to only the installer files, instead exposing the entire bucket including subsequently added sensitive data.
facts: ["Bucket IAM policy grants roles/storage.objectViewer to \"allUsers\" making all objects publicly readable by anyone on the internet.","Bucket belongs to provision-general-operations project and uses legacy IAM roles predating uniform bucket-level access.","Storage Transfer Service account project-255935175694@storage-transfer-service.iam.gserviceaccount.com has objectAdmin and legacyBucketReader permissions.","Project-level principals (projectEditor, projectOwner, projectViewer) have legacy bucket and object permissions.","Six distinct IAM bindings exist on the bucket including the public allUsers binding that enables the security vulnerability."]

title: Step 03 - Sequential Story Generation with Dependency Rules
narrative: Step 03 implements sequential story generation that transforms approved epics into implementation-ready stories with detailed acceptance criteria. The step enforces two critical principles that prevent common anti-patterns: just-in-time entity creation (each story creates only the database tables/entities it needs rather than creating all 50 tables in Epic 1 Story 1) and zero forward dependencies (Story 1.2 cannot require Story 1.3 to be completed first). Stories follow the standard user story format with As a/I want/So that structure and include specific Given/When/Then acceptance criteria that are independently testable. Each story must be sized for single dev agent completion and deliver clear user value - stories like "Set up database" or "Create all models" are rejected as having no user value. The process is collaborative with user review after each story before appending to the output file. Epics are processed sequentially maintaining proper numbering (Epic N Story M) and template structure compliance. The final document integrates all previously completed sections (Requirements Inventory, FR Coverage Map, Epic List) with the newly generated epic and story details, creating a complete implementation blueprint ready for development teams.

title: Virtual Memory Statistics Show Active Memory Pressure and Reclamation
narrative: Virtual memory statistics provide granular insight into the memory pressure identified earlier. The system has processed over 849 million translation faults, indicating heavy memory access patterns requiring page table lookups. With only 221MB of free pages available, the system maintains roughly 4.7GB of active memory and 5.2GB of inactive memory still resident in RAM. The 5.6GB of wired memory cannot be paged to disk and includes kernel structures and locked application memory. The system has aggressively purged over 27 million pages (cumulatively ~434GB) throughout its uptime, demonstrating continuous memory pressure requiring reclamation of purgeable memory. Anonymous pages consuming 9.2GB represent application heap and stack memory not backed by files. The low ratio of file-backed pages (1GB) to anonymous pages (9.2GB) suggests memory-intensive applications dominating RAM usage rather than file I/O caching.

title: Meta Skill Security Ecosystem Evaluation Against Factory's 8-Gate Topology
narrative: Created comprehensive security evaluation mapping Jeffrey Emanuel's security ecosystem to Factory's 8-gate pipeline topology with constraint to enhance gates without restructuring them. The analysis identified Factory's security gap: no defense against prompt injection in spec bodies or command-level interception inside the SDK sandbox. ACIP's trust hierarchy addresses the injection gap by distinguishing "instructions from orchestrator" versus "data from specs/patterns/lessons" - critical for the Coder agent which has bypassPermissions and full Bash access. The compact 150-token trust preamble fits within Factory's thin-agent budget while defending against malicious spec bodies, poisoned patterns, compromised lessons, and inject-via-parent-bead attacks. DCG addresses the command interception gap - SDK sandbox prevents escaping the box, but DCG prevents destroying things inside the box. Factory's fail-fast philosophy requires forking DCG to fail-closed on timeout rather than fail-open. UBS fills a correctness gap between Biome (style/formatting) and Semgrep (security) by catching bugs like resource leaks and null pointer dereferences. The evaluation rejected SLB because Factory's value proposition is "submit spec, walk away" - requiring human approval for dangerous commands contradicts autonomous operation. Meta Skill rejected despite architectural interest because the PRD explicitly excludes CASS/Meta Skill integration at MVP, custom licensing creates legal risk, and Thompson sampling is scoped to Phase 3 vision. Each recommendation includes gate mapping, cost analysis, priority, approach, and risk mitigation.

title: Factory Architecture Decisions - Technology Stack and NFR-Driven Design
narrative: The Factory architecture document maps 125 requirements (90 FR + 35 NFR) to concrete technical decisions driven by non-functional requirements. Performance NFRs mandate flat agent topology for minimal dispatch overhead, thin CLI client over running daemon for 500ms response target, and prompt caching. Reliability NFRs drive Supervisor daemon with crash recovery, PID-based singleton enforcement, and append-only telemetry. Security NFRs enforce agent isolation with SDK sandboxing and read-only scenario evaluator access. The technology stack commits to Bun runtime (native TypeScript, built-in testing, HTTP server) and Anthropic Claude SDK exclusively with no abstraction layers. Agent boundary modules absorb SDK changes while orchestration logic remains stable. Telemetry uses JSONL event streams analyzed via DuckDB. Configuration follows XDG Base Directory compliance. The three-level error handling system provides nested containment: intra-agent limits prevent runaway turns, intra-gate retries handle transient failures, and pipeline-level timeouts enforce global budgets. The architecture acknowledges high complexity requiring all subsystems (Pipeline, Supervisor, Memory) to ship together for the product to function autonomously.
facts: ["Architecture document completed 8 steps based on PRD, product brief, and technical research documents","90 functional requirements organized across 8 capability areas: Specification, Pipeline, Supervision, Memory, Run Management, CLI, Configuration, Pattern System","35 non-functional requirements across Performance, Reliability, Security, Integration, and Observability categories drive architectural decisions","Technology stack uses Bun runtime exclusively with Anthropic Claude Agent SDK v0.2.37 (pre-1.0)","Flat agent topology enforced by SDK limitation where subagents cannot spawn subagents","Dual database strategy: Dolt for versioned data, DuckDB for analytical queries over JSONL telemetry","Three-level error handling loop system: intra-agent (turn/budget limits), intra-gate (max retries), pipeline-level (wall-clock timeout, total budget cap)","Single operator design with Unix socket implicit auth, no multi-user support at MVP"]

title: Factory commands use two-tier architecture with lightweight wrappers loading full agent personas
narrative: Factory command architecture revealed a two-tier pattern: commands in .claude/commands/ serve as lightweight activation wrappers rather than containing full agent logic. Each command file uses YAML frontmatter to disable model invocation and includes critical activation instructions that direct Claude to load the complete agent persona from module-specific directories under _bmad/. The dev agent command, for example, instructs Claude to load _bmad/bmm/agents/dev.md containing the full persona, menu system, and behavioral instructions. This architecture separates command registration (Claude Code's .claude/commands/ for discoverability) from agent implementation (_bmad/ modules for organization and maintenance). The activation pattern emphasizes strict adherence: "NEVER break character until given an exit command" and marks instructions as CRITICAL=TRUE. This design leverages Claude Code's native skill system while organizing complex agent behaviors in a modular structure, fundamentally different from pi-mono's isolated subprocess approach where each agent runs independently without shared context.

title: Factory agent architecture uses XML-structured personas with workflow execution and interactive menus
narrative: Full agent definition files revealed the factory's sophisticated persona-based architecture. Agents use XML-structured definitions containing multi-step activation sequences, rich personas with defined roles/identities/communication styles, interactive menu systems, and specialized handlers for workflow execution. The dev agent exemplifies the pattern: loads configuration into session variables at activation, enforces strict test-driven development discipline (no proceeding with failing tests, tasks executed in order from story files, completion marked only when tests pass), and processes menu items through handlers that can dynamically load workflow definitions from _bmad/core/tasks/workflow.xml. This stateful, interactive architecture operates entirely within Claude Code's main conversation context, maintaining rich session state and user interaction throughout execution. The contrast with pi-mono's subprocess isolation is stark: factory agents are conversational interfaces with persistent state and configuration, while pi-mono agents are stateless workers that receive task strings and return output strings. The factory design leverages Claude Code's native context management and skill composition, making subprocess isolation fundamentally incompatible with the architecture's requirements for shared state, configuration, and interactive menu systems.

title: Security Architecture and Business Model Strategy for MCP Service
narrative: A critical security and architecture review surfaced five major infrastructure concerns requiring strategic decisions. The most severe issue (Hole 2) involves validating malicious MCP responses without actually protecting users—validating bad input instead of blocking it, creating a business-killing security gap that needs immediate brainstorming. For API reliability (Hole 1), the retry model was deemed acceptable as it aligns with current industry norms for LLM APIs. Security controls (Hole 3) will be developer-configurable, offering options like MTLS and deny-all mode with sensible defaults for different use cases, giving end users control over their security posture. API downtime resilience (Hole 4) represents a critical dependency risk where Claude API outages could halt business rule processing for millions of businesses, requiring a sophisticated solution with high-throughput ingestion, risk-based scoring, and tiered vetting (fast-track for known actors, extended validation for new users). Infrastructure scaling concerns (Hole 5) may necessitate building a custom DNS server in Rust optimized for this specific use case. The overarching business model preserves FOSS principles with low-friction adoption to enable community vetting and iteration, while offering paid enterprise tiers for organizations that need additional security bureaucracy, allowing developers to self-implement if they don't want a trust anchor.

title: Rate limiting rules not executing - no session tracking or counter activity in logs
narrative: A targeted search of the mod_security audit log for evidence of rate limiting rule execution revealed complete absence of any rate limiting activity. Despite 15 POST requests to /gl/ng/api/ccsp/guarlife/submit with valid JSESSIONID cookies, none of the configured rate limiting rules (900001-900004) left any trace in the audit log. This indicates the rules are not executing at all, rather than executing but failing to block. The LocationMatch directive targeting "/gl/(ng|ap/ng)/api/.*/submit" should have matched all 15 requests, but the rules within that block produced no logging output, counter increments, or blocking actions. Possible causes include: SecRuleEngine directive not taking effect within LocationMatch contexts, phase ordering issues preventing rule execution, or syntax errors in the rule definitions that prevent parsing. The complete silence of the rules suggests a fundamental configuration or compatibility issue preventing the mod_security rule chain from executing inside the LocationMatch directive.

title: Comprehensive analysis of beads_rust frozen architecture and Agent Flywheel foundation
narrative: Comprehensive research on beads_rust revealing its architectural philosophy as a frozen classic implementation that preserves the SQLite+JSONL hybrid architecture. Unlike the original beads which is evolving toward GasTown, br maintains a stable 20k line codebase (versus 276k in Go beads) with explicit user control over all operations. The dual storage approach uses SQLite for immediate query performance and JSONL export for git collaboration with clean line-based merging. Critical design principle: non-invasive operation with no automatic git commands, hooks, or background daemons. All sync and import operations require explicit user action. Supports multiple output modes including JSON and Robot flags for AI agent consumption. The ready-work detection filters issues by status (open), dependencies (unblocked), and deferral state. Significantly, creator Jeffrey Emanuel (same person from initial "jeffrey emanual research" request) built Agent Flywheel tooling around this architecture, suggesting br's design directly supports the factory project's agent workflows. This explains why preserving the classic architecture matters versus following beads' evolution to GasTown.

narrative: Investigated the ZeroClaw project website at https://zeroclaw.org/ to understand the framework's capabilities and organizational structure. ZeroClaw is a performance-focused AI assistant framework written in Rust, engineered for ultra-lightweight operation with startup times under 10ms and a compact 3.4MB binary size. The framework provides memory safety guarantees through Rust and implements a pluggable architecture using Rust trait systems, enabling seamless integration with 22+ AI providers (including OpenAI, Anthropic, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, and Ollama) and multiple communication platforms (Telegram, Discord, Slack). The technical architecture comprises three distinct layers: a trait-based async Core Layer for foundational operations, an AI Adapter Layer providing unified API abstraction across providers, and a Channel Layer handling message routing and event management. The project is open source under the MIT License, hosted at github.com/openagen/zeroclaw, and the website is built with Next.js and Tailwind CSS. The website contains no references to theonlyhennygod or zeroclawlabs.ai, no commercial pricing structure, and no attribution to specific team members or company entities, suggesting this is a community-driven open source project rather than a commercial venture.

title: Attacker IP 207.242.44.6 exhibits automated enumeration pattern with spoofed browser signature
narrative: Identified attacker traffic pattern from IP 207.242.44.6 showing clear signs of automated reconnaissance and data enumeration. The traffic occurs in synchronized bursts with multiple API endpoints accessed within the same second, revealing scripted automation rather than human browsing behavior. The sequence systematically probes agency portal capabilities: checking email registration status, querying agent information, accessing document APIs, searching case statuses and applications, then logging out. The consistent 150-byte responses on successful requests suggest the API returns error messages or empty result sets rather than actual data, indicating the attacker may not have valid session context despite apparent success codes. Authentication failures on sensitive endpoints (secure-email, document favorites) and CMS access denial demonstrate access controls are partially effective. The attacker attempts evasion by spoofing a legitimate Chrome browser signature and proper referer headers to mimic genuine user traffic and bypass basic bot detection. The extremely fast response times (1-50ms) and same-second timing across multiple requests confirm automated tooling. With 4,461 total submits identified earlier, this pattern repeated thousands of times represents significant reconnaissance activity attempting to map the application's API surface, test authorization boundaries, and potentially identify vulnerabilities or data exposure points.

title: Traefik routing correctly configured for OTEL Collector on os.hexxa.dev
narrative: The Traefik configuration on the OTEL Collector container is correctly set up for telemetry ingestion. All traffic to os.hexxa.dev (with any path including /v1/traces, /v1/metrics, /v1/logs) is routed to the Collector's port 4318, which is the standard OTLP HTTP endpoint. The configuration includes proper TLS termination with Let's Encrypt certificates, automatic HTTP-to-HTTPS redirection, and gzip compression for responses. This explains why curl tests to the endpoint returned HTTP 200 - Traefik is successfully routing requests to the Collector. Since the reverse proxy layer is working correctly, the ingestion problem must be either in the OTEL Collector's receiver configuration (whether it's properly listening and configured to accept OTLP data on port 4318) or in the claude-code application (whether the OTEL SDK is actually initialized and sending telemetry data).

title: Factory PRD - Supervised Autonomous Software Production System
narrative: The Factory PRD defines a supervised autonomous software production system designed to ensure "done" means verified and running. The architecture separates concerns: the Supervisor daemon manages long-lived process orchestration (health monitoring, killing stuck agents, enforcing controls), while the stateless Pipeline enforces eight mandatory verification gates ensuring artifacts actually compile, test, lint, secure-scan, and pass scenarios before completion. The Memory layer sits outside both, providing persistent learning through lessons auto-loaded by tag matching, reusable patterns via @ composition, and reject history with full narratives. The frontmatter-as-constraint-system uses a 15-property spec schema with smart defaults (quick/standard/tenacious effort presets, quick/standard/strict gate profiles, fail_fast/iterate/escalate reject policies) validated pre-flight and in-flight. Every factory-built artifact inherits natural language invocation by default. The reject-revise loop is treated as a core workflow, not an edge case, with bead narratives providing actionable next-step guidance and parent_bead lineage preventing repeated mistakes. Success is measured by autonomous closure rate (≥3 components/week) and reject-to-success conversion (>70% on second attempt).

title: Dark Factory Agent Atomicity Research Goals Established
narrative: The _factory-core project is investigating fundamental limits of atomicity for building Claude coding agents that operate without human intervention (the "dark factory"). The research focuses on three key questions: (1) What is the smallest atomic unit that can be isolated and composed? (2) How precise can the execution environment be made to ensure deterministic behavior? (3) Can the factory architecture support replacing absolutely any component - tools, prompts, context, execution environment - while maintaining composability? This work aims to create a fully modular system where coding agents can be assembled from interchangeable parts, enabling rapid experimentation and evolution of agent capabilities without manual intervention.

title: iTerm2 Visual Daemon Design Documented
narrative: The session documented an architectural pattern addressing the environment management constraints discovered through earlier Claude Code introspection. Rather than attempting to bridge Claude Code's subprocess-per-invocation model with local environment complexity (mise, persistent shell state, git integration), the design embraces separation of concerns: the local iTerm2 terminal maintains rich stateful context while Claude Code operates in clean isolation, consuming outputs and producing artifacts without managing environment state. This decomposed execution model directly responds to the subprocess architecture research showing working directory as the only persistent state across Bash tool invocations. The visual feedback system leverages iTerm2's Python API for dynamic profile switching, creating persistent visual cues (background colors, badges) that survive across all terminal interactions—addressing Brian's problem of starting work in wrong or dirty branches when hooks and skills proved unreliable. The six-state profile strategy maps git cleanliness (clean/dirty), branch type (main/feature), and Claude Code activity status to distinct visual appearances, making state immediately obvious through terminal background color rather than requiring command execution. The documented iTerm2 API capabilities reveal sophisticated monitoring options: ScreenStreamer for output analysis, PromptMonitor for command boundary detection, CustomControlSequenceMonitor for application-specific signals—providing multiple integration paths. The AutoLaunch daemon approach ensures continuous background operation independent of Claude Code sessions. By identifying existing FOSS tools (it2 CLI wrapper, iterm2-triggers patterns), the design can leverage community solutions rather than reimplementing API access. The file edit removing implementation specifics (polling interval, state machine logic, completion status) suggests a pivot from detailed design to high-level reference documentation, keeping the memory focused on architectural principles and API capabilities rather than implementation minutiae. This pattern—using external persistent systems for stateful concerns while keeping Claude Code focused on code transformation—offers a template for handling the broader category of local environment integration challenges.

title: Claude Agent SDK architecture and execution model documented
narrative: Successfully retrieved the Claude Agent SDK overview documentation from platform.claude.com. The SDK bridges interactive development (CLI) with production automation needs, offering identical capabilities in a library form suitable for CI/CD, custom applications, and programmatic workflows.

title: Environment Variables Do Not Persist Across Claude Code Commands
narrative: Empirical testing confirmed that environment variables do not persist across Claude Code bash command executions. A test variable set to alive_1771867152 in one command returned to its default value DEAD when checked in the next command, despite executing within the same Claude session. This behavior stems from Claude Code's subprocess execution model: each bash command spawns a new zsh process that sources the shell snapshot file, inheriting the snapshot's environment but discarding any modifications made during execution. The snapshot file itself is read-only during command execution and not updated with runtime changes. The only mechanism for environment persistence is SessionStart hooks writing to CLAUDE_ENV_FILE, which updates the snapshot for all future commands in that session. This isolation prevents environment pollution between commands but requires explicit configuration via hooks for persistent environment state like API keys, PATH modifications, or tool-specific environment variables.
facts: ["Environment variable CC_PERSIST_TEST set to alive_1771867152 in first command","Same variable reads as DEAD (unset default value) in second command executed moments later","Each Claude Code bash execution spawns fresh zsh subprocess sourcing snapshot file from scratch","Environment modifications within a command do not propagate to subsequent command executions","Only SessionStart hooks can persist environment variables via CLAUDE_ENV_FILE mechanism"]

title: AbstractUser authentication security model analyzed

title: CSR bot uses orchestrator pattern with polling, evaluation, and Slack interaction
narrative: The successful CSR bot demonstrates a proven orchestrator pattern for automation with human-in-the-loop workflows. The Orchestrator class wires together five specialized components and manages their coordination through async event loops. The poll loop queries ClickUp at configurable intervals, discovering new tickets and queueing them for processing. Each ticket flows through StateManager tracking (QUEUED → EVALUATING → EVALUATED → APPROVED/ESCALATED/SKIPPED → CLOSED), with the Evaluator performing AI analysis and SlackBot posting results with interactive buttons. The human-in-the-loop mechanism uses Slack callback wiring where user button clicks trigger Orchestrator handlers that update state and invoke the Executor for approved actions. A separate auto-execute loop checks every 60 seconds for tickets past their deadline, automatically executing unless manually held. Critical resilience features include DB connectivity checking before evaluations (deferring work when DB is down with Slack notifications), startup recovery for tickets stuck mid-evaluation from interrupted runs, and graceful shutdown via signal handlers. This architecture provides a proven template for the new Rackspace ticket daemon requiring similar polling, evaluation, Slack interaction, and human-in-the-loop patterns.

title: Claude Code Shell Environment Configuration
narrative: Investigation into Claude Code's shell execution environment revealed that commands run in zsh subprocesses that first source environment snapshot files. Each command execution creates a subprocess (PID 35325) parented by the main Claude session (PPID 33714), sources a timestamped snapshot file to initialize the environment, disables extended globbing, and then executes the requested command. This snapshot-based approach preserves environment state across command invocations while maintaining isolation.

title: ZeroClaw Code Quality Standards and CLI Entry Point
narrative: Examined zeroclaw's main.rs entry point (2033 lines total) revealing strict code quality standards and comprehensive CLI structure. The codebase enforces 100% safe Rust through #![forbid(unsafe_code)] directive, completely eliminating unsafe blocks and providing strong memory safety guarantees without escape hatches. Code quality is maintained through clippy::all and clippy::pedantic lint sets with a carefully curated allow list for 30+ specific rules deemed too noisy (module_name_repetitions, too_many_lines, missing_errors_doc, etc.). The CLI is built with clap parser framework supporting shell completion generation for bash, fish, zsh, powershell, and elvish shells. Emergency stop functionality implements four escalation levels providing graduated incident response: kill-all terminates all agent processes, network-kill cuts network access, domain-block restricts specific domains, and tool-freeze disables tool execution while preserving conversation. The onboard command provides flexible initialization with interactive wizard mode for full guided setup, quick setup for minimal configuration, force overwrite flag for non-interactive reinstall, and targeted channel reconfiguration for repair workflows when only communication setup is broken. Main.rs imports and organizes 30+ modules spanning the full platform architecture including agent execution loop, security layers (policy, sandboxing, secrets), provider abstraction, tool execution, communication channels, hardware peripherals, memory subsystems, observability stack, and skillforge for dynamic capability expansion. Temperature parameter validation constrains LLM temperature to 0.0-2.0 range with parse-time validation preventing invalid configurations. The modular structure maintains separation between binary (main.rs) and library (lib.rs) code while re-exporting shared command enums (ChannelCommands, CronCommands, HardwareCommands, etc.) to maintain single source of truth across boundaries.

title: Tightened JSESSIONID Rate Limits Based on Attack Pattern Analysis
narrative: The security.conf rate limiting parameters were tightened based on observed attack behavior analysis. The slower decay rate punishes session rotation behavior by maintaining elevated counters longer, making it harder for attackers to reset their limits by cycling sessions.

title: Protocol Architecture Already Specifies JSON-RPC 2.0 Over REST

title: site_user_sessions Table Schema Shows Session-to-User Mapping Design
narrative: Investigation examined site_user_sessions table schema to understand the intended session attribution mechanism. This table is specifically designed to map session tokens to authenticated usernames with user type classification. The session_token field (nvarchar 300) would store the JSESSIONID or equivalent session identifier, while the username field provides direct attribution to the authenticated user. The user_type field allows filtering for CSR-specific sessions versus subscriber sessions. Critically, the expire_time field (datetime) explains why the smoking gun session (JSESSIONID node05p3qwzlq19k1o4w8gq1vln8q100714.node0) is no longer present in the table - sessions are automatically purged after expiration. At investigation time (20:38), the attack session last seen at 15:08 had expired over 5 hours ago and was cleaned from the table. This confirms that site_user_sessions WAS the correct database table for direct session attribution, but the temporal gap between attack and investigation resulted in evidence loss due to standard session cleanup procedures. The investigation must therefore rely on temporal correlation and circumstantial evidence rather than direct database session mapping.

title: Mise Version Manager Incompatibility with Claude Code
narrative: Discovered root cause of mise (polyglot version manager) failures in Claude Code environments. Mise uses shell activation patterns that set up shims, modify PATH, and export dynamic environment variables based on .mise.toml configuration files. Claude Code's subprocess isolation model actively breaks this by resetting environment state between each bash invocation. Even if mise activates successfully in one command, the next command gets a fresh bash session with no mise context, causing tools to be "not found" despite being properly installed and configured in the normal terminal. The CLAUDE_ENV_FILE workaround doesn't solve this because mise's shims are too dynamic to pre-export, and per-directory environment variables change based on working directory context. PreToolUse hooks can't fix this either since they run in isolated subprocesses. This represents a fundamental architectural incompatibility between mise's stateful activation model and Claude Code's stateless command execution.
facts: ["Mise relies on eval \"$(mise activate)\" shell hooks that modify PATH and export environment variables for activated tool versions","Claude Code subprocess isolation resets mise activation context between commands causing tool shims to disappear","Mise shims are dynamically generated based on installed versions and cannot be frozen in CLAUDE_ENV_FILE due to their changing nature","Per-directory environment variables from .mise.toml files never reach Claude Code commands due to environment variable reset behavior","Claude Code PreToolUse hooks fire before bash commands but run in separate subprocess so environment modifications don't persist to actual command"]

title: Security Researcher Validation Framework and Internal Security Guide
narrative: Created comprehensive internal security documentation establishing a systematic framework for PIAM engineers to validate security researcher contacts. The guide addresses a critical operational security need: distinguishing legitimate responsible disclosure from social engineering and extortion attempts. The 6-dimension validation framework provides concrete evaluation criteria across identity verification (LinkedIn profile, phone number, company website), email infrastructure (SPF/DMARC records, mail server type, geographic consistency), claim specificity (generic vs. technical details, org knowledge), contact patterns (spray-and-pray vs. targeted channels), researcher footprint (media coverage, CVEs, conference talks, published research), and behavioral signals (secure channel requests, payment demands, disclosure timelines). Two detailed case studies contrast a fake researcher (Bugswagger/Oumeir Saifedeen using mass email platform with generic claims) against a legitimate researcher (Adam Burns from Blackveil Security with verified identity, hardened email infrastructure, specific technical claims about exposed cloud storage containing CI/CD credentials, and knowledge of company's former name). Includes practical OSINT commands for quick validation (whois, dig for DNS records, Google dorking) and a printable quick reference card. Document explicitly notes the Adam Burns/Blackveil situation remains active as of February 23, 2026, and will be updated with final outcomes.

title: Claude Code Shell Snapshot Mechanism
narrative: Investigation revealed Claude Code's shell environment preservation strategy through snapshot files. Each command execution sources a timestamped snapshot that first clears aliases, then reconstructs the complete shell environment including functions (zsh hooks, color support, mise integration), shell options, custom aliases, and a comprehensive PATH. A notable feature is the conditional ripgrep alias that provides fallback to Claude's bundled version, ensuring tool availability even when not system-installed. The PATH spans over 1KB and includes mise-managed tools for development (node, python, go, docker, kubernetes utilities), demonstrating deep integration with the user's development toolchain.

title: SDK introspection harness implemented to programmatically observe Claude Code execution model
narrative: Implemented a comprehensive TypeScript-based introspection harness that uses the Claude Agent SDK to programmatically observe Claude Code's execution model from within. The tool runs as a two-phase experiment: Phase 1 executes a carefully designed prompt that instructs the SDK-spawned Claude Code instance to run 9 diagnostic commands capturing shell identity, environment variables (particularly Claude-specific ones like CLAUDECODE, MISE_SHELL, MISE_YES), PATH configuration, mise version, process arguments, and shell snapshot directory state. Phase 2 resumes the same session and tests whether exported environment variables persist across the resume boundary - a critical test of the shell snapshot/restore mechanism. The harness instruments the agent lifecycle using in-process hook callbacks (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure) that capture tool execution events synchronously within the Node.js process, contrasting with CLI hooks that run as separate shell subprocesses. These hooks receive typed input objects containing tool names, inputs, outputs, session IDs, and working directories, enabling precise timeline reconstruction. All SDK messages are captured including the init message (revealing session ID, CWD, model, permission mode, available tools, MCP servers), assistant messages (showing tool_use blocks), and result messages (containing cost, token usage, duration, turn count). The tool generates dual-format reports: a complete JSON dump with all structured data for programmatic analysis, and a human-readable Markdown report with 8 sections analyzing different execution aspects - init message details, tool execution timeline table, full diagnostic command outputs, session management comparison, environment persistence test results, execution summary with costs, hook lifecycle statistics showing PreToolUse/PostToolUse pair counts per tool, and message type distribution. This harness enables SDK-level introspection of Claude Code's runtime behavior, complementing the previous shell-based introspection work by providing an API-driven observability layer with programmable hooks and structured data capture.

Phase 2 resumes session to test environment variable persistence","Hook instrumentation via PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure callbacks captures tool execution lifecycle in-process - different from CLI shell-based hooks","Diagnostic commands specifically probe: SHELL/0 variables, ps output, CLAUDE_ENV_FILE/CLAUDECODE/MISE_SHELL/MISE_YES environment variables, full env dump, PATH contents, mise version, process args, shell snapshot directory","Environment persistence test exports SDK_PERSIST_TEST variable in Phase 1, then checks its value in Phase 2 after session resume to verify shell state continuity","Data collection captures: SDKSystemMessage init with session_id/cwd/model/tools/mcp_servers, tool events timeline with timestamps/phases/inputs/outputs, all bash command outputs keyed by command, SDKResultMessage with cost/tokens/duration, complete message stream","Report generation outputs dual formats: JSON file with full structured data (timestamps, tool events, all messages), Markdown file with 8 analysis sections (init message, tool timeline, diagnostic outputs, session management, env persistence, execution summary, hook lifecycle, message type breakdown)","Hook callbacks fire in Node process (not shell subprocesses), receiving typed HookInput with hook_event_name/tool_name/tool_input/tool_response/session_id/cwd fields","Options configuration uses: permissionMode bypassPermissions, allowDangerouslySkipPermissions true, allowedTools Bash/Read/Glob, maxTurns 15, hooks object with callback matchers, resume parameter for Phase 2","Message streaming via AsyncGenerator pattern - for await loop over query() result processes SDKMessage union types with discriminated type/subtype fields"]

title: Comprehensive codebase attack surface analysis mapped all security gaps to source code
narrative: Comprehensive source code analysis across all GLIC repositories mapped every attack endpoint to its implementation, revealing systemic security gaps throughout the monolithic Java/J2EE application. The application consists of 10+ Maven modules assembled into a single WAR with two distinct technology stacks running in the same context: legacy Struts 1.3 for CSR/agent admin functions and modern Spring MVC for product submission APIs. Analysis of all 10 attack vectors identified critical architectural vulnerabilities: zero rate limiting exists anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via regex search across all Java files), Spring MVC REST endpoints lack CSRF protection entirely, SAML signature validation is disabled via code comment, and payment authorization is intentionally bypassed for agency-channel submissions via regex exclusion. Authentication implementation analysis revealed that core security classes (LoginCsrUserAction, AbstractAuthenticateUserWithPasswordTransaction) are duplicated across three separate repositories, requiring synchronized fixes. The account lockout mechanism implements a weak 5-attempts-per-24-hours policy that automatically resets the counter after the time window expires, allowing perpetual low-rate credential stuffing. Most significantly, the analysis revealed that all auto-issuance and underwriting logic resides in the external CAESER backend service, not in the web application code, meaning the 233 fraudulent policies were approved by CAESER's underwriting engine which lacks bot detection and velocity controls. The document provides complete file paths and line numbers for all security-relevant code across the codebase, enabling targeted remediation.

title: Fixed Dumpio LogLevel Override in httpd.conf
narrative: A configuration ordering bug prevented mod_dumpio from producing POST body logs despite being loaded and configured. The root cause was a LogLevel directive at line 235 of httpd.conf that set module-specific log levels to "warn log_debug:info" without including dumpio:trace7. Since this directive was processed after postdata-logging.conf loaded the dumpio module and set its log level, the httpd.conf setting effectively overrode and suppressed dumpio's trace7 output. Apache's LogLevel directive allows space-separated module-specific levels (format: "default_level module1:level1 module2:level2"), and the fix appended "dumpio:trace7" to the existing module-specific settings. This ensures dumpio maintains trace-level logging even after the line 235 directive is processed. The remaining dumpio configuration fragments in httpd-modules.conf were also removed to complete the consolidation into postdata-logging.conf. With this fix, mod_dumpio will now output POST request bodies to the error log at trace7 verbosity level while other modules maintain their assigned log levels.
facts: ["httpd.conf line 235 LogLevel directive was overriding dumpio:trace7 setting from postdata-logging.conf","Modified LogLevel from \"warn log_debug:info\" to \"warn log_debug:info dumpio:trace7\" at line 235","Removed all remaining dumpio configuration fragments from httpd-modules.conf","Global LogLevel remains at debug (line 194), module-specific LogLevel at line 235 now includes dumpio:trace7"]

title: Module Loading Separation - Early Load in httpd-modules.conf
narrative: The forensic logging configuration was refactored to separate module loading from configuration directives. Apache modules must be loaded before any directives from those modules can be used, and the Include order in httpd.conf determines when modules become available. By moving LoadModule directives for mod_dumpio and mod_log_forensic into httpd-modules.conf (included at line 55), the modules are now loaded early in the configuration parsing sequence, before the main httpd.conf body and before conf.d wildcard includes. The basic dumpio directives (DumpIOInput On, DumpIOOutput Off) were also moved to httpd-modules.conf to keep module loading and basic setup together. More complex configuration like ForensicLog and CustomLog directives remain in postdata-logging.conf which is included later. This separation ensures modules are available when their more advanced directives are parsed, preventing "module not loaded" errors that could occur if configuration directives appeared before the LoadModule statements. The configuration retains clear removal markers for post-investigation cleanup.
facts: ["Added LoadModule dumpio_module and LoadModule log_forensic_module to httpd-modules.conf","Moved DumpIOInput On and DumpIOOutput Off directives to httpd-modules.conf alongside module loading","httpd-modules.conf included at line 55 of httpd.conf, ensuring modules load before most configuration processing","Separates module loading (early in httpd-modules.conf) from log format configuration (later in postdata-logging.conf)","Configuration marked with \"INCIDENT INVESTIGATION - REMOVE AFTER COMPLETE\" comment for cleanup identification"]

title: ACFS - Agentic Coding Flywheel Setup Multi-Agent Environment Architecture

title: CRITICAL: Jenkins Master Key, Credentials Database, and GCP Service Account Keys Exposed
narrative: Targeted search across all transfer operation logs uncovered catastrophic credential exposure. The most critical finding is Jenkins secret.key - this 64-byte file is the master encryption key that Jenkins uses to encrypt all secrets stored in its credentials database. With this key, an attacker can decrypt every password, API token, SSH key, and certificate stored in the exposed credentials.xml file (containing 29KB of encrypted credentials). The exposure includes a complete GCP service account key in ADC JSON format for ansible-deploy@elabs-test-293219.iam.gserviceaccount.com, granting programmatic access to the elabs-test-293219 GCP project visible in the infrastructure. Additional exposure includes GCP credentials database, access tokens database containing OAuth2 tokens, Jenkins instance identity key, and API token configuration. The combination of secret.key and credentials.xml represents a complete compromise of the Jenkins security model - every secret Jenkins has ever encrypted can now be decrypted by anyone who downloaded these files during the exposure window. The service account credentials provide persistence even after password resets, as service account keys remain valid until explicitly revoked. File timestamps ranging from 2018 to 2027 indicate this Jenkins instance accumulated credentials over multiple years, all of which are now potentially compromised. This exposure necessitates complete credential rotation across all systems that Jenkins had access to, service account key revocation, and forensic analysis to determine if the files were accessed during the 7-month exposure period.
